exercises.name: The Proof Point Answer
exercises.rule: Prove the trait with one specific moment, not a claim.
exercises.why: Interviewers discount adjectives. A concrete moment with your role, action, and result is the only evidence they can score.
exercises.objective: Train the user to answer behavioral interview questions by proving the target trait with one specific, evidence-rich moment instead of asserting it.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: claimed traits without a concrete example MUST drag thinking_quality. Reward answers where the specific action and result make the trait believable on their own.
exercises.coach_insight: Pick one moment and tell it so specifically the interviewer could retell it — where you were, what you did, what changed. The trap is pluralizing: "I always" and "I often" turn evidence back into a claim.
common_failure_modes: Asserts the trait ('I'm very proactive') without a single specific moment to back it
Describes what the team or company did instead of the speaker's own actions
Blurs several incidents into a habitual 'I usually...' instead of one concrete story
Ends without a result anyone could verify or score
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the specificity and ownership of the single chosen moment — whether the actions are unmistakably the speaker's and the result is checkable — over the overall polish of the delivery.
exercises.retry_objective: Replace every general self-description from the first attempt with a specific action, number, or result.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate behavioral interview questions that name a role and setting with real stakes and target one evaluable trait, so unproven claims become visible. Each prompt must suit a 75-120 second spoken STAR-style answer drawn from the user's real experience.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "Tell me about a time you took initiative without being asked."
You're in a final round for a consulting role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you influenced someone senior to change course."
You're interviewing for your first full-time job. Answer: "Tell me about a time you had to learn something quickly under pressure."
You're interviewing for a research assistant position. Answer: "Tell me about a time your attention to detail actually mattered."
You're interviewing at a startup. Answer: "Tell me about a time you delivered with almost no direction or resources."
You're in a promotion panel at your company. Answer: "Tell me about a time you took ownership of a problem outside your role."
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "Tell me about a time you responded well to tough feedback."
You're interviewing for a team lead role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you kept a group on track when motivation dipped."
You're interviewing for a customer-facing role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you turned an unhappy person into an advocate."
You're interviewing for a data analyst job. Answer: "Tell me about a time the numbers told you something nobody wanted to hear."
You're interviewing for a scholarship. Answer: "Tell me about a commitment you kept when it would have been easier to quit."
You're interviewing for an operations role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you improved a process everyone else had accepted."

exercises.name: The Failure Debrief
exercises.rule: Name the failure, own it, show what changed.
exercises.why: Failure questions test honesty and arc. Interviewers listen for whether you bury the mistake, blame others, or land a real lesson.
exercises.objective: Train the user to walk a failure story through a clean arc — context, mistake, response, change — without burying the failure or the lesson.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the answer must move through context, failure, response, and change in order. A buried mistake, deflected blame, or missing final lesson MUST drag structure.
exercises.coach_insight: Say "I" at the mistake and get there fast — then spend more time on what you changed than on how it went wrong. The trap is over-explaining the setup to delay the confession; interviewers hear the stall.
common_failure_modes: Spends most of the answer on setup and only glances at the actual mistake
Frames the failure as bad luck or someone else's fault
Jumps from the failure straight to a moral, skipping what the speaker actually did next
Lands a lesson so generic it could follow any story ('communicate more')
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight ownership language and the specificity of the change enacted afterward more heavily than the drama or scale of the failure itself.
exercises.retry_objective: Tighten the arc: name the failure within the first two sentences and land the lesson as the final beat.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate failure-question interview prompts that name a role and setting where the stakes of admitting a mistake are real. Each prompt must invite one specific failure from the user's own experience and suit a 75-120 second spoken answer with a clear story arc.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "Tell me about a time you failed at something that mattered to you."
You're in a final round at a startup. Answer: "Tell me about a project of yours that didn't work out. What happened?"
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "Describe a time you missed a goal you set for yourself."
You're interviewing for a team lead role. Answer: "Tell me about a decision you got wrong and what it cost."
You're interviewing for an engineering job. Answer: "Tell me about a time something you built or set up failed at the worst possible moment."
You're in a promotion conversation with your director. Answer: "Tell me about your biggest miss in the last year."
You're interviewing for a consulting role. Answer: "Tell me about a recommendation you made that turned out to be wrong."
You're interviewing for a nonprofit role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you overcommitted and let someone down."
You're interviewing for your first job. Answer: "Tell me about a time you were unprepared. What did you do next?"
You're interviewing for a sales role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you failed to win someone over when it really mattered."
You're interviewing for a fellowship. Answer: "Describe a failure that changed how you approach your work."
You're interviewing for a product role. Answer: "Tell me about a launch or deadline you missed and what you changed after."

exercises.name: Across the Table
exercises.rule: State their side fairly before you defend yours.
exercises.why: Conflict questions measure maturity, not victory. Representing the other person fairly proves judgment; villainizing them proves the opposite.
exercises.objective: Train the user to answer conflict questions by representing the other side fairly before explaining their own actions and the resolution.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the other party's view must be stated fairly before the user's own case. Villainizing, flattening, or skipping the other side MUST drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Make their case sound so fair they'd nod if they heard it — then show what you did and how it resolved. The trap is the token nod: "I see their point, but..." used as a runway for your own victory lap.
common_failure_modes: Gives the other side one throwaway sentence before pivoting to self-defense
Paints the other person as unreasonable, political, or incompetent
Tells the conflict as a victory story with no concession or change on the speaker's side
Never says how the disagreement actually resolved or what the relationship became
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight how much genuine strength the speaker grants the opposing view and whether the resolution shows movement on their own side, rather than adjudicating who was right.
exercises.retry_objective: Restate the other person's position at full strength before defending your own, and name one thing you would do differently.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate conflict-question interview prompts naming a role and setting where a real disagreement with a specific person is expected. Each prompt must draw on the user's own experience, make the other party's perspective essential to a good answer, and suit 75-120 seconds spoken.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate. How did it end?"
You're interviewing for a team lead role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you and the person in charge saw a problem differently."
You're interviewing for a consulting role. Answer: "Tell me about a time someone you were doing work for pushed back hard on you."
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "Describe working closely with someone whose style clashed with yours."
You're interviewing at a startup. Answer: "Tell me about a time you had to commit to a decision you argued against."
You're interviewing for a project manager job. Answer: "Tell me about a time two people on your team were in conflict and you stepped in."
You're interviewing for a research role. Answer: "Tell me about a time your interpretation of the evidence was challenged."
You're interviewing for a customer success role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you had to say no to someone important."
You're in a promotion panel. Answer: "Tell me about your hardest working relationship and what you did about it."
You're interviewing for a volunteer leadership role. Answer: "Tell me about a time you changed your mind during a disagreement."
You're interviewing for an engineering job. Answer: "Tell me about a debate over the right way to do something that you lost. Was the outcome right?"
You're interviewing for a teaching position. Answer: "Tell me about a time someone criticized your approach in front of others."

exercises.name: Why This, Why You
exercises.rule: Give one specific reason only you could give.
exercises.why: Motivation answers fail by being interchangeable. One concrete, personal reason tied to this role beats five reasons anyone could recite.
exercises.objective: Train the user to answer motivation questions with one specific, personal reason made concrete — not a resume summary or generic praise for the organization.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the motivation must be one concrete, personal reason the listener could repeat back. Generic praise, stacked vague reasons, or a resume recap MUST drag clarity.
exercises.coach_insight: Give the one reason with your fingerprints on it — a moment or detail no other candidate could borrow, tied to this exact role. The trap is stacking three safe reasons; together they say nothing about you.
common_failure_modes: Lists multiple interchangeable reasons instead of committing to one
Praises the organization in phrases that could be lifted from its website
Recaps the resume instead of explaining the motive
Offers a reason that would fit any similar role with the names swapped
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the single reason is unmistakably this speaker's — anchored in a lived moment and this specific opportunity — over how enthusiastic or comprehensive the answer sounds.
exercises.retry_objective: Cut to one reason and anchor it in a specific moment or detail only you could supply.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate motivation-question interview prompts naming a role and setting where the interviewer is testing genuine intent. Each prompt must push the user toward one personal, concrete reason from their own life and suit a 60-90 second spoken answer with no stacked constraints.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "Why do you want this internship, and why now?"
You're in a final round at a startup. Answer: "Why us instead of a bigger, safer company?"
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "Why this field? Trace the moment it became your plan."
You're interviewing for a role in a field new to you. Answer: "Why step into something new instead of building on what you already know?"
You're interviewing for a nonprofit job. Answer: "Why this mission, and what have you already done about it?"
You're interviewing for a consulting role. Answer: "Why consulting — beyond the answer everyone gives?"
You're interviewing for a teaching fellowship. Answer: "Why do you want to teach, and why these students?"
You're interviewing for a scholarship. Answer: "What do you want to build with this, five years out?"
You're interviewing for your first job. Answer: "What kind of work makes you lose track of time, and why?"
You're in a promotion conversation. Answer: "Why do you want this role instead of staying where you're strong?"
You're interviewing for a research position. Answer: "Why this question? Why does it deserve years of your life?"
You're interviewing at a company you admire. Answer: "What specifically about our work made you apply?"
You're opening an interview. Answer "Tell me about yourself," then end with one genuinely compelling question for the interviewer.

exercises.name: One Strength, One Proof
exercises.rule: One strength, one proof, then stop talking.
exercises.why: Strength answers die by accumulation. A single claim backed by one sharp example lands; a list of adjectives cancels itself out.
exercises.objective: Train the user to deliver a strength answer as one claim and one concrete proof, then stop — no stacked strengths or trailing qualifiers.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: one strength, one concrete proof, a clean stop. Stacked strengths, trailing qualifiers, or restating the point after the proof lands MUST drag conciseness.
exercises.coach_insight: Claim in one sentence, one vivid proof, then a full stop — the silence after the example does the selling. The trap is the nervous coda: "and I'm also..." quietly refunds everything you just earned.
common_failure_modes: Names two or more strengths, weakening each
Keeps the proof habitual ('in every project I...') instead of one specific moment
Restates the claim or summarizes after the example has already landed
Trails off into qualifiers or a bonus mini-strength instead of stopping
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight what happens after the proof — whether the speaker actually stops — and how tightly the example demonstrates the exact strength claimed, more than how impressive the strength itself is.
exercises.retry_objective: Keep the strongest claim-proof pair from the first attempt and cut everything after the proof lands.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate strength-question interview prompts naming a role and setting where one relevant strength is enough. Each prompt must invite a single claim backed by one specific example from the user's experience and suit a 60-90 second spoken answer that rewards stopping early.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "What's your greatest strength?" One strength, one example, done.
You're interviewing for a team lead role. Answer: "What would your teammates say you're best at?"
You're interviewing for a sales role. Answer: "What makes you good at winning people over? Prove it fast."
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "What one ability will carry you through this program?"
You're in a final round at a startup. Answer: "What do you do better than most people at your level?"
You're interviewing for an analyst role. Answer: "What's your sharpest skill, and when did it last change an outcome?"
You're interviewing for a customer-facing job. Answer: "What's your superpower with difficult people?"
You're in a promotion panel. Answer: "What strength justifies giving you more responsibility?"
You're interviewing for your first job. Answer: "What's one thing we'd notice about your work in the first month?"
You're interviewing for a fellowship. Answer: "What strength shows up in everything you've done so far?"
You're interviewing for an engineering role. Answer: "What part of this job will you be immediately strong at?"
You're interviewing for a leadership program. Answer: "Name the strength you're known for. Give the moment that proves it."

exercises.name: The Unhedged Weakness
exercises.rule: A real weakness, no hedging, plus the fix underway.
exercises.why: Hedged weakness answers read as evasive. Naming a real gap plainly, then showing the fix in motion, is what builds trust.
exercises.objective: Train the user to name a real weakness plainly, without hedging or humblebragging, and show the concrete fix already in motion.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the weakness must be real and stated without hedges or disguised strengths. Softeners like "sometimes maybe", perfectionism cliches, and humblebrags MUST drag conciseness.
exercises.coach_insight: Name a gap a former teammate would instantly recognize, in one plain sentence, then show the fix already in motion. The trap is the costume: a strength dressed as a weakness reads as evasion, not modesty.
common_failure_modes: Offers a disguised strength ('I'm a perfectionist', 'I care too much')
Softens the gap with hedges like 'sometimes', 'a little', or 'maybe'
Names a real weakness but no concrete fix currently in progress
Spends longer justifying or explaining away the weakness than owning it
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the plainness of the admission and the specificity of the in-progress fix; a genuinely risky weakness owned cleanly should outscore a safe one wrapped in caveats.
exercises.retry_objective: State the weakness in one unhedged sentence, then spend the saved time on the concrete fix underway.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate weakness-question interview prompts naming a role and setting where honesty carries real risk. Each prompt must push the user to name a genuine gap from their own experience plus an active fix, and suit a 60-90 second spoken answer with no room for hedging.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a competitive internship. Answer: "What's your biggest weakness?" No fake weaknesses allowed.
You're in a final round at a startup. Answer: "What would the last person who oversaw your work say you need to work on?"
You're interviewing for a team lead role. Answer: "What feedback have you gotten more than once?"
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "What part of this program will be hardest for you?"
You're interviewing for a consulting role. Answer: "Where do you fall short under pressure?"
You're in a promotion conversation. Answer: "What would hold you back in this role, honestly?"
You're interviewing for your first job. Answer: "What skill are you actively working on right now, and how?"
You're interviewing for a research position. Answer: "What's a habit that has hurt your work, and what replaced it?"
You're interviewing for a customer success role. Answer: "What kind of situation still throws you off?"
You're interviewing for an engineering job. Answer: "What's a real gap in your skills for this role?"
You're interviewing for a fellowship. Answer: "Tell me something you tried to improve and are still not good at."
You're interviewing for a project manager role. Answer: "When did your weakness last cost your team something?"

exercises.name: The Honest Why
exercises.rule: Give the real reason, then the proof you acted on it.
exercises.why: Interviewers discount polished motives. The reason that survives follow-up is the real one, backed by something you have already done about it.
exercises.objective: Train the user to explain why they want something or chose something with their real, defensible motive — honestly traced, mixed feelings included — and backed by action already taken, not borrowed platitudes.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the stated motive must be honest and defensible under follow-up. Borrowed platitudes, suspiciously tidy motives, or reasons unbacked by any prior action MUST drag thinking_quality.
exercises.coach_insight: Give the reason that would survive two more "whys" — mixed feelings included — plus one thing you've already done that proves it. The trap is the answer you think they want; borrowed motives collapse on the first follow-up.
common_failure_modes: Recites a borrowed platitude ('I'm passionate about impact') as the motive
Tells a suspiciously tidy origin story with no trade-offs or mixed feelings
Cites no prior action that backs the stated reason
Shifts the motive mid-answer without acknowledging the change
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the stated motive is corroborated by actions already taken and honest about trade-offs, over how inspiring or role-appropriate the motive sounds.
exercises.retry_objective: Swap any polished or borrowed reason for the truer one, and name one thing you have already done that proves it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate why-questions about motivations and past choices, naming a role and setting where the interviewer will probe the answer for authenticity. Each prompt must invite the user's real reasoning about their own decisions and suit a 75-120 second spoken answer.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a marketing internship. Answer: "Out of everything you could have applied to, why this?"
You're interviewing for your first full-time job. Answer: "Why did you pick what you've spent the last few years learning or doing?"
You're interviewing at a small nonprofit. Answer: "Why do you care about this cause?"
You're interviewing for a retail management job. Answer: "Why this industry? Most people just fall into it."
You're in a final round at a fintech startup. Answer: "You have safer options than this. Walk me through why you're here."
You're interviewing for a graduate program. Answer: "Plenty of programs cover this. Why ours, really?"
You're in a promotion panel. Answer: "Why do you want to manage people instead of staying where you're strongest?"
You're interviewing for a nurse practitioner role. Answer: "Why choose work like this over paths that pay more?"
You're interviewing with a nonprofit board for an executive director post. Answer: "Why take this on now, at this point in your career?"
You're in a final round for a consulting job. Answer: "Parts of your background point in different directions. Which is the real you, and why are you here?"
You're interviewing to rejoin a team you once left. Answer: "Why come back? And why should we believe it will stick?"
You're interviewing for a PhD program. Answer: "Is this a calling or an escape from the job market? Convince me you know the difference."

exercises.name: The Composed Curveball
exercises.rule: Pause, name your approach, then reason aloud at a steady pace.
exercises.why: Curveball questions test composure before content. A beat of silence and a steady, step-by-step answer reads as judgment; a rushed scramble reads as panic.
exercises.objective: Train the user to meet curveball, hypothetical, and trade-off questions with a deliberate pause, a named approach, and steady structured reasoning aloud toward a defensible call.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: the answer must open with a deliberate pause and hold a steady rate while reasoning aloud. A rushed start, a filler-flooded scramble, or an accelerating panic MUST drag pacing.
exercises.coach_insight: Take a real two-second pause, say how you'll approach it, then walk steady steps to one call. The trap is filling the silence — "um, great question" — and speeding up as confidence drops; the rush reads as panic.
common_failure_modes: Starts talking instantly, plugging the silence with fillers instead of a real pause
Speeds up or turns breathless as the answer progresses
Circles through considerations without naming an approach or ordered steps
Hedges to the end of the clock and never commits to a call
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the opening seconds and rate stability across the whole answer — the pause, the named approach, the held tempo — more than whether the final call is the optimal one.
exercises.retry_objective: Take a full two-second pause before speaking, and hold one steady pace from your first reasoning step to your final call.
exercises.prompt_rules: Generate curveball, hypothetical, and trade-off interview questions naming a role and setting where no rehearsed answer exists. Each prompt must force live reasoning toward a defensible choice and suit a 75-120 second spoken answer delivered at a composed pace.

prompts.text: You're interviewing for a campus tour guide job. Answer: "A parent asks you something you don't know. What do you do, and what makes that the right call?"
You're interviewing for an operations job. Answer: "Would you rather ship a day late or ship with one known small bug? Pick one."
You're interviewing for a retail lead role. Answer: "Two customers need you at once and you're alone on the floor. Who gets you first?"
You're interviewing for a junior analyst job. Answer: "Rough guess: how many coffee shops does this city need? Reason it out for me."
You're interviewing for a product manager role. Answer: "You can stop customers from leaving or bring in new ones this quarter, not both. Which one, and why?"
You're in a promotion panel for engineering manager. Answer: "Your best engineer wants to quit the day before a launch. What's your first move?"
You're interviewing for a hospital administrator role. Answer: "Budget forces a cut: staff training or new equipment. Choose."
You're interviewing for an MBA program. Answer: "Your team's startup idea is failing mid-semester. Pivot or push on? How would you decide?"
You're interviewing for a chief of staff job. Answer: "Your CEO is about to announce a number you think is wrong. You have sixty seconds with her. Go."
You're in a board interview for a director seat. Answer: "The CEO is beloved but underperforming. What does the board do, and when?"
You're in a final round for a strategy role. Answer: "Our biggest client, 40% of revenue, demands a discount that erases the margin. Walk me through your call."
You're interviewing for a research director post. Answer: "Halfway through, the data contradicts the hypothesis your funding depends on. What now?"
